## PRE-LAUNCH: Foundation (T-30 to T-21) — 4 posts

---

### Post 1 — Problem Post (T-30 | 2026-04-15)

```
Day: T-30
Date: 2026-04-15
Platform: LinkedIn
Post type: problem
Copy:
How much of your last sprint was spent reading code you didn't write before you felt safe to change it?

I've asked this question to about 40 engineers in the past year. The average answer is 6 hours per sprint.

That's not onboarding. These are experienced engineers — 2, 5, 10+ years in. They just work in codebases where the code doesn't explain itself.

The consequences are real:
- Onboarding takes weeks instead of days
- Code reviews slow down because reviewers don't understand the context
- Incident response is guesswork until someone who "knows this part" is found
- Changes get made cautiously — too cautiously — because blast radius is unknown

I've been thinking about what it would take to make a codebase as navigable as a database with good documentation. More soon.

What's your number? How many hours per sprint do you spend understanding before acting?
Visual descriptor: Plain text card — quote in large font: "6 hours per sprint, just to understand the code they were about to change." White background, minimal styling.
CTA: none (comment prompt)
```

### Post 2 — Founder Story (T-28 | 2026-04-17)

```
Two years ago I joined a codebase with 180,000 lines of TypeScript.

First week: asked "where does auth happen?" to three different people.
Got three different answers.
All three were partially right.

That's when I started thinking about this problem.

I've been building the answer for the past year.
Visual descriptor: Founder headshot or candid working photo. Authentic, not staged.
```

---

### Post 3 — Problem Education (T-25 | 2026-04-20)

```
Day: T-25
Date: 2026-04-20
Platform: LinkedIn
Post type: problem
Copy:
Why "just read the code" doesn't scale after 50,000 lines.

Code navigation breaks down for three reasons that compound:

1. **Indexing problem.** The brain can't index 50,000+ lines. You remember what you've read recently, not what exists. Someone who's been on the team 3 years knows their modules; they don't know the whole system.

2. **Dependency invisibility.** In a language like TypeScript, what a function affects is determined by who imports it — and that's not visible at the call site. You can read a function perfectly and still not know what changes when it changes.

3. **Context decay.** Code you understood last quarter becomes unfamiliar code after 6 weeks away. The understanding doesn't persist — you have to re-derive it.

These three forces mean that "just read the code" is a strategy that gets slower as a codebase grows, not faster. The tooling hasn't caught up.

I think that's about to change. More soon.
Visual descriptor: Diagram showing the three forces: "indexing problem," "dependency invisibility," "context decay" arranged as a loop with "codebase opacity" at the center.
CTA: none
```

---

### Post 4 — Behind-the-Scenes (T-22 | 2026-04-23)

```
Day: T-22
Date: 2026-04-23
Platform: Instagram
Post type: problem
Copy:
We've been building something for the past year.

The core question: what if asking "where does X happen in this codebase?" took 12 seconds instead of 45 minutes?

Not a web search. Not a chatbot that makes things up. Something that actually knows what's in the repo — and can prove it.

We've been indexing codebases, testing answers, counting wrong paths. The answer rate is getting good. More soon.
Visual descriptor: Photo of a whiteboard with early design sketches — diagram of "question → retrieval → answer → citation" flow, hand-drawn. Authentic, unpolished. Coffee cup in corner for atmosphere.
CTA: none
```

### Post 6 — Problem Thread (T-17 | 2026-04-28)

```
Day: T-17
Date: 2026-04-28
Platform: Twitter/X
Post type: problem
Copy:
[7-tweet thread]

Tweet 1: The best senior engineer I know spent 20 minutes explaining "how authentication works here" to a new hire last Tuesday. They've explained the exact same thing to the last 4 new hires. That's not a people problem. That's a tooling problem. 🧵

Tweet 2: Here's the issue: codebases don't document themselves. Code shows you what it does, not why, not how it connects, not what you should know before touching it.

Tweet 3: So understanding lives in people's heads. Which means every answer to "where does X happen?" requires finding the right person, interrupting them, and hoping their memory of that part is current.

Tweet 4: The consequence: senior engineers spend 30-40% of their time as "human documentation." Answering questions they've answered before. Questions that should have a persistent, searchable answer.

Tweet 5: AI tools were supposed to fix this. GitHub Copilot Chat, ChatGPT — they help with writing code. But ask them "where does auth happen in *this* repo?" and they'll give you a plausible answer that's wrong. They don't know your repo. They can't cite a line.

Tweet 6: The missing piece: retrieval. Not "LLM knows code in general" but "LLM knows *your* code specifically" — grounded in what's actually in the repo, with citations you can click and verify.

Tweet 7: That's what we've been building. Reveal coming in about 2 weeks. If this problem sounds familiar — follow along.
Visual descriptor: No image needed for thread. Tweet 1 may include a simple stat card: "Senior engineers spend 30-40% of time as human documentation."
CTA: "RT if this resonates"
```

```
The tool: indexes your Git repo and answers questions about it — with citations pointing to real file:line ranges. Every answer is verifiable.

Details at the link. 14 days until we launch publicly.
```

### Post 8 — Product Reveal (T-10 | 2026-05-05)

```
Day: T-10
Date: 2026-05-05
Platform: LinkedIn + Twitter/X
Post type: reveal
Copy (LinkedIn — 200 words):
Introducing RepoBrain — a codebase intelligence layer for engineering teams.

Connect a GitHub repo. RepoBrain indexes it across three retrieval strategies — semantic, lexical, and structural — and lets you ask questions about it in natural language.

The key difference: every answer cites a real file:line in your repo. Not a plausible-sounding guess. A citation you can click and verify.

"Where does authentication happen?" → answer with auth.ts:47, middleware.ts:23, session.ts:89. Click any one. You're at the line.

Beyond conversations: blast radius analysis, natural language structural search, pattern detection, architecture diagrams, specialist agents, and 15 features total.

This is the tool I needed three years ago. Now it exists.

10 days until public launch. Early access is open at [link]. Founding price locks in at $29/mo for the first 100 teams.

Copy (Twitter/X — 280 chars):
Introducing RepoBrain.

Ask questions about any codebase → get answers with real file:line citations.

Three retrieval strategies. No hallucinated paths. 15 features.

Launching May 15th. Early access open now: [link]

Visual descriptor: Clean product UI screenshot — dark theme, question typed in chat bar, answer visible with 3 inline citation badges (auth.ts:47 etc.) highlighted in blue.
CTA: [early access link]
```

```
Platform: LinkedIn
```
```
Post type: reveal
```

```
The "where does X happen" problem has no good solution today.

Here's what engineers do instead — and what RepoBrain does differently. (Swipe →)

[5-slide carousel]

Slide 1 copy: "Where does authentication happen in this codebase?" — The question every engineer asks. The question no tool answers reliably.
Slide 2 copy: What you do today: Grep for keywords. Trace imports manually. Ask a colleague. Average time: 45 minutes. Reliability: depends on who you asked.
Slide 3 copy: RepoBrain indexes your repo with 3 parallel retrieval strategies. Semantic: finds conceptually similar code. Lexical: keyword matching. Structural: follows symbol relations (calls, imports, uses).
Slide 4 copy: The answer arrives with citations — auth.ts:47, middleware.ts:23, session.ts:89. Click any one. You're at the line. Every claim is verifiable in one click.
Slide 5 copy: RepoBrain launches tomorrow. Index your first repo free. Founding price ($29/mo Pro) ends Friday. [link]
```

```
RepoBrain is the middle ground. Index your repo. Ask questions. Get cited answers — file:line citations pointing to real code you can open and verify.

Tomorrow at 12:01 AM PST we go live. Free tier includes one repo and four core features. Pro ($29/mo) unlocks everything — 15 features, 10 repos, specialist agents.
```

```
RepoBrain is live.

A year of building, two months of beta testing, and 15 features later — it's open.

RepoBrain indexes your Git repository and answers questions about it with real file:line citations. Ask "where does auth happen?" Ask "what calls this function?" Ask "find error handlers without retry logic." Every answer points back to your actual code. Every claim is one click from verification.

This is the tool I needed three years ago when I joined a 180,000-line codebase and spent my first two weeks just trying to understand it. It exists now.

Go to repobrain.dev, authorize GitHub, and index your first repo for free. You'll have your first cited answer in under 10 minutes.

If you want all 15 features — blast radius, pattern detection, architecture diagrams, specialist agents for code review and security audits, Git timeline, and more — Pro is $29/mo through Friday, May 17th. After that it goes to $49/mo.

[repobrain.dev]
```

### Post 16 — Top Question (T+3 | 2026-05-18)

```
Day: T+3
Date: 2026-05-18
Platform: LinkedIn
Post type: result
Copy:
The most common question we've gotten since launching:

"Does it work on monorepos?"

Yes. We indexed a 400,000-line TypeScript monorepo during beta. Indexing took 22 minutes. Retrieval quality was strong — structural search had to traverse more relations, but the ranker handled it well.

The honest caveat: very large monorepos (1M+ lines) haven't been stress-tested at scale. We know retrieval degrades when the context window gets crowded. That's the next engineering problem we're solving.

What's your question? Reply below or email hello@repobrain.dev — we read everything.
Visual descriptor: Quote card with the question "Does it work on monorepos?" in large font. RepoBrain brand treatment, dark background.
CTA: comment prompt — "What's your question? Reply below."
```

### Post 20 — Feature Spotlight: Blast Radius (T+12 | 2026-05-27)

```
Day: T+12
Date: 2026-05-27
Platform: Twitter/X
Post type: feature
Copy:
RepoBrain feature I underestimated when building it: Blast Radius.

Ask: "If I change the User type, what breaks?"

It traverses symbol relations — every function that uses User, every file that imports it, every call that depends on its shape — and returns a list of affected files.

It doesn't run your compiler. It reads the symbol graph.

For TypeScript refactors: this is the diff-before-the-diff. You see the blast before you pull the pin.

repobrain.dev
Visual descriptor: Screen recording — "User" typed into blast radius input → list of 12 affected files appears, sorted by depth of dependency. 15-second recording.
CTA: [repobrain.dev]
```

### Post 22 — Feature Spotlight: Specialist Agents (T+16 | 2026-05-31)

```
Day: T+16
Date: 2026-05-31
Platform: LinkedIn
Post type: feature
Copy:
Most people discover RepoBrain through Code Conversations. The feature that surprises them later: the specialist agents.

Three agents come with Pro: Code Review, Security Audit, and ADR Generator. Each one reads your codebase the same way — through the index, with citations — but focuses on a specific domain.

Security Audit is the one that gets the strongest reaction. Point it at a module: it identifies injection risks, authentication gaps, token handling issues, and hardcoded secrets — all cited to the exact file:line. Not a static scan — a reasoning agent that understands context.

We used it on RepoBrain's own codebase. It found the issue described in our architecture audit (SSL validation disabled, `rejectUnauthorized: false`). Two lines, 10 minutes to fix. Without the agent, it sat undiscovered for two months.

[repobrain.dev — Pro features available on the $29/mo tier]
Visual descriptor: Split view: left = Security Audit UI showing issue list with severity badges; right = code viewer open at the offending line (auth.ts or equivalent). Clean, dark theme.
CTA: [repobrain.dev]
```

```
Two weeks post-launch. Here's what the actual build status looks like.

What's working well: retrieval quality on TypeScript repos, citation accuracy, response times under 3 seconds.

What we're fixing: monorepo support at scale, the occasional false positive in structural search, re-index latency.

What's coming next: shareable citations, webhook-based auto re-index, better progress UI during initial indexing.

Being honest about the current state matters to us. You're using an early version of something that's going to get much better. We appreciate the patience — and the bug reports.
```

### Post 26 — Underused Feature: Git Timeline (T+24 | 2026-06-08)

```
Day: T+24
Date: 2026-06-08
Platform: LinkedIn
Post type: feature
Copy:
The most underused RepoBrain feature: Git Timeline.

Most people index a repo and go straight to Code Conversations. Git Timeline gets discovered later — and it's worth knowing about earlier.

Git Timeline shows you the semantic shape of a repository's history. Not a raw git log — a visual representation of which features emerged in which commits, how modules grew, and where the most volatile parts of the codebase are.

Practical uses:
- "Which part of this codebase has changed most in the last 6 months?" → shows you where to read carefully
- "When did the authentication module get added?" → names the commit, the engineer, and the files
- "What was this service before it became what it is now?" → narrates the transformation

If you've been skipping it: it's in the left sidebar under "Timeline." Worth 5 minutes.

Do you use the Git Timeline? What would make it more useful? Reply below.
Visual descriptor: Annotated screenshot of the Git Timeline view — semantic commit clusters highlighted, with callouts explaining what each cluster represents.
CTA: comment prompt — "Do you use Git Timeline? What would make it more useful?"
```

### Post 28 — 30-Day Report (T+30 | 2026-06-14)

```
Day: T+30
Date: 2026-06-14
Platform: LinkedIn
Post type: result
Copy:
30 days since we launched RepoBrain.

[TRACTION NEEDED — full 30-day numbers: repos indexed, Pro teams, questions answered total, retention rate, churn if any]

What we learned that surprised us:

1. The use case we didn't expect: engineers using RepoBrain on repos they wrote 2+ years ago and don't fully remember. "It's like having a conversation with my past self's code."

2. Onboarding is faster than we thought — the first useful answer usually comes in session 1. The "aha moment" is always the citation: clicking a badge and landing on the exact line.

3. Security Audit gets used more than Code Conversations on first install. Engineers want to know what's wrong before they go deep.

Month 2 begins tomorrow. The three things we're building: shareable cited answers, webhook auto-reindex, and better cross-repo intelligence for monorepo workspaces.

Thank you for the first 30 days.

[repobrain.dev]
Visual descriptor: 30-day metrics card or dashboard screenshot. Month-over-month growth chart if available. Professional but honest — no vanity numbers.
CTA: none (or [repobrain.dev] for new readers at the bottom)
```
